storm.summary_layperson: No storm threat today — skies are sunny and dry, though NWS forecasters note isolated afternoon thunderstorm chances returning Thursday and Friday.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Cool Long Island Sound water (70°F versus 82°F air) maintains a moderate marine layer that would weaken any approaching storms — though none are in the area today.
storm.detail_html:
The atmosphere over the region is almost completely stable today. CAPE is effectively zero, radar shows no storm cells within range, and there are no active NWS watches, warnings, or advisories.

The NWS Area Forecast Discussion (OKX, issued 3:50 PM) highlights an isolated strong-to-severe thunderstorm and flash flood threat on Thursday and Friday afternoon and evening, driven by a surface trough in a marginally unstable environment with 0–6 km shear of 20–35 kt. That threat is not in play today.

The marine layer is present and moderately protective: Long Island Sound water is 70°F versus inland air at 82°F, a 12°F gap that builds a stable layer over the water. When storms do approach from the SW–NW (the most common track), this layer frequently weakens or splits cells before they reach the cove, reducing local risk compared to inland Westchester. Protective mechanism is active; its effect today is academic since there is nothing to mitigate.

For tonight there is nothing to watch. If you are planning outdoor activities Thursday or Friday, revisit the forecast closer to those dates — arrival timing and intensity will sharpen considerably by Wednesday night.

hourly.shift_note: steady, near peak — sunset at 8:15 PM

wind.tactics_blurb: SSW sea breeze channels along the Field Point / west shore — favor the right side of the beat. Wind builds to 9–10 kt by 7–8 PM then softens after the 8:15 PM sunset; expect noticeably lighter air past 9 PM.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Today is a textbook Regime A pure thermal sea breeze. Inland air at 82°F versus Sound water at 70°F gives a 12.2°F land–water temperature gap — well above the 5°F activation threshold. The synoptic gradient is negligible (NWS calling SW at 3 mph), so the sea breeze is carrying the entire load with no synoptic tailwind to augment it.

Direction is still maturing. HRRR's raw forecast holds around 171–194° (due S through the evening), but the western-LIS station ensemble is already reading SSW at ~206° — a 28° divergence. The fetcher applies a 15° maturation correction to give a corrected forecast of 186–209° SSW through the window. Trust the observed SSW as the working direction; HRRR is running behind, as it typically does late afternoon on Regime A days. RYC itself is at SSE 167° right now, slightly backed — expect continued clockwise veer to ~SSW 195–205° by 18:00.

For the cove course, SSW flow at 195–210° channels slightly along the western shore (Field Point, Indian Harbor, Belle Haven side), delivering more consistent pressure on the right side of the beat. Boats that tack right early on the first upwind leg — starboard toward Field Point — should find clean lifts on the final approach to the windward mark. The left (eastern / Greenwich Point / Tod's Point) side carries light shelter from the peninsula at this angle.

Shift character: steady oscillations of ±10° once the SB locks in around 18:00. The 17:00 hour may still feel like the late ramping phase — continuing rightward veer and building pressure — so one more persistent right shift is likely before it settles. Once established, the SB should be well-behaved through the peak window (18:00–20:00).

Sunset is at 8:15 PM (20:15). Regime A collapses within 30–60 minutes of sunset as the land stops heating. Wind should start softening around 20:15–20:30, easing from a peak of 9–10 kt down toward 7 kt or less by 22:00. Any race that runs past 21:00 should budget for lighter-than-predicted conditions; the HRRR extended outlook holds 7–9 kt through 22:00, but the model tends to be slow on Regime A collapse.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 (Western LIS, over-water winds) offline — UConn ERDDAP 404 error; no over-water observations available
